45: A direct revelation: 'Not one word of all the good promises that the LORD had made to the house of Israel had failed; all came to pass.' Joshua's testimony to YHWH's promise-keeping is the OT's most comprehensive affirmation of divine faithfulness to the covenant. Paul applies the same principle to the gospel: 'For all the promises of God find their Yes in him. That is why it is through him that we utter our Amen to God for his glory' (2 Corinthians 1:20). Christ is the fulfillment of every OT promise — not just some, but all. The land-promises of Joshua are included: their ultimate referent is the new creation inheritance (Romans 4:13: the promise that he would be heir of the world; Hebrews 11:16: they desire a better country, a heavenly one).
1: A type: the heads of the Levitical fathers’ houses came to Eleazar the priest and to Joshua and to the heads of the fathers’ houses of the tribes of Israel at Shiloh in Canaan and spoke to them. The Levites come last — after all twelve tribes have received their allotments — to claim the cities YHWH promised through Moses (Numbers 35:1-8). The Levitical claim is not territorial conquest but inherited entitlement, received through the covenant promise. Hebrews 7:11-14 argues that the Levitical priesthood is insufficient and gives way to the Melchizedekian order in Christ — yet the principle of priestly provision by divine command is fulfilled in Christ’s priestly provision for his people, distributed not through territorial cities but through his own indwelling presence.
2: A type: the command YHWH gave through Moses — that we be given cities to dwell in, with pasturelands for our livestock. The appeal to Moses’s command at Shiloh (the central sanctuary) frames the Levitical claim as a matter of covenant right, not tribal negotiation. The priestly tribe does not take; it receives what YHWH commanded the other tribes to give. In the NT, the priesthood of all believers (1 Peter 2:9) receives its provision through Christ’s own body: “he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?” (Romans 8:32). The Levitical receiving-from-others becomes the model of the church receiving all things through Christ.
3: A type: by YHWH’s command the Israelites gave the Levites these cities and their pasturelands from their own inheritances. The act of the twelve tribes giving from their own portions to support the priestly tribe is the covenant structure of mutual provision: the Levites serve the whole nation, and the whole nation provides for the Levites. Paul applies this principle to gospel ministry: “Let the one who is taught the word share all good things with the one who teaches” (Galatians 6:6). The Levitical support principle — those who minister at the altar live from the altar (1 Corinthians 9:13-14) — is the type of the church’s provision for those who proclaim the gospel.
4: A type: the lot fell for the Kohathites — the clan of Aaron the priest received thirteen cities from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin. The Aaronite Kohathites, the high-priestly clan, receive cities in Judah’s territory — the tribe from which David and the Messiah will come. The high priesthood and the royal tribe are geographically neighbors; Jerusalem itself (later Judah’s royal city) is adjacent to the priestly cities. In Christ, the royal-priestly union is personalized: he holds both offices in one person (Hebrews 7:1-3: after the order of Melchizedek, “king of righteousness” and “king of peace”).
5: A type: the rest of the Kohathites received ten cities from the tribe of Ephraim, Dan, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. The non-Aaronite Kohathites carry the holy furniture of the tabernacle (Numbers 4:4-20) — the ark, the table of showbread, the menorah, the altars — all covered and carried on poles through the wilderness. The bearers of the sacred objects of God’s dwelling receive cities in the central and northern tribes, positioned throughout the land as custodians of the tabernacle tradition. In the new covenant, the bearers of the sacred mystery — the church — carry the gospel (“treasure in jars of clay,” 2 Corinthians 4:7) throughout the nations as the Kohathites carried the sacred furnishings throughout the land.
6: A type: the Gershonites received thirteen cities from the tribes of Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan. The Gershonites carried the fabrics and coverings of the tabernacle (Numbers 4:24-26) — the curtains, veils, and coverings that formed the visible structure of YHWH’s dwelling. Their cities in the northern tribes (Issachar, Asher, Naphtali) placed the tabernacle-covering tradition in the region that will become “Galilee of the Gentiles” (Isaiah 9:1-2; Matthew 4:15-16) — the area where Jesus conducted most of his ministry. The fabric-bearers’ inheritance was in the territory where the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us (John 1:14).
7: A type: the Merarites received twelve cities from the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun. The Merarites carried the structural elements of the tabernacle — the frames, bars, pillars, and bases (Numbers 4:31-33). Their inheritance includes the Transjordanian tribes (Reuben and Gad) who are separated from the main body of Israel by the Jordan. The Merarites’ priestly presence in the separated territories is the type of the church’s ministry in the margins: the priestly community is sent not only to the central, accessible tribes but to those on the far side of the boundary. Acts 1:8 — “to the ends of the earth” — is the NT expansion of the Merarite model.
8: A type: the Israelites assigned these cities with their pasturelands to the Levites by lot, as YHWH had commanded through Moses. The lot-assignment confirms the divine character of the Levitical distribution: even the priestly city allocations are determined by YHWH’s direction, not tribal preference. The pastoral provision — cities and pasturelands (migrash) — ensures that the priests can both minister in the sanctuary and sustain their households. Christ’s provision for his servants includes both the ministry of his word and the material sustenance needed to carry it out (Matthew 10:9-10: workers deserve their wage; Luke 10:7).
9: A type: the cities assigned from the tribes of Judah and Simeon — the firstfruits of the priestly city allocation. Judah’s role as host to the Aaronite priestly cities anticipates Judah’s hosting of Jerusalem — the city of the temple, the city of David, the city of Christ’s death and resurrection. The priestly cities embedded in Judah’s territory lay the groundwork for the Jerusalem sanctuary tradition that culminates in the temple of Christ’s body (John 2:19-21).
10: A type: the cities going to the Aaronite Kohathites (the first lot among the Levites). The “first lot” going to the Aaronites is the covenant priority structure: the Aaronite high priesthood takes precedence in the Levitical distribution. Hebrews 5:4 notes that “no one takes this honor for himself, but only when called by God, just as Aaron was” — the Aaronite priority in the lot reflects the divine call that precedes priestly service. Christ was called by the divine voice at his baptism (Matthew 3:17: “This is my beloved Son”) and transfiguration (Matthew 17:5) — his high priesthood rests on divine appointment, not human selection.
11: A type: Kiriath-arba (the city of Arba, the father of Anak) — that is, Hebron — in the hill country of Judah, with its surrounding pasturelands. Hebron is given to the Levites as one of their cities — the same city just given to Caleb as his personal inheritance (Joshua 14). The resolution is v12: the fields and villages go to Caleb; the city itself (within its walls) goes to the Levites. The co-inhabitation of Hebron by Caleb (the faithful warrior) and the Levitical priests (the covenant ministers) models the complementary vocations of warrior-faith and priestly service that are united in Christ’s person as both priest and king.
12: A type: the fields of the city and its villages they gave to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his possession. The distinction between the city proper (given to the Levites) and the fields/villages (given to Caleb) preserves both inheritances within the same territory. The tension is resolved not by exclusion but by complementary allocation. In Christ, the royal and priestly functions are not competitive but complementary: “He is the atoning sacrifice for our sins” (priestly, 1 John 2:2) and “the ruler of the kings of the earth” (royal, Revelation 1:5) — both offices fully inhabited, neither diminishing the other.
13: A type: Hebron is a city of refuge for the manslayer. The Levitical cities include six cities of refuge (Josh 20) — Hebron, Shechem, Kedesh, Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan — where anyone who killed unintentionally could flee from the blood-avenger. Hebrews 6:18: “we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us” — the six cities of refuge are the type of Christ as the one asylum where those guilty before the divine law may find safety. The city of refuge was Levitical: the priestly city, not the military city, is the place of life-saving asylum.
14: A type: Libnah with its pasturelands, Jattir with its pasturelands — additional Aaronite cities in Judah. The distribution of the priestly cities throughout Judah ensures that priestly presence is not concentrated only at the central sanctuary but dispersed through the territory. The church as “a royal priesthood” (1 Peter 2:9) is similarly dispersed: not gathered in one sacred location but scattered through every city, town, and village, carrying priestly mediation through prayer, proclamation, and embodied witness.
15: A type: Eshtemoa with its pasturelands, Holon with its pasturelands — Aaronite cities continuing. The pastoral dispersal of the Levitical cities throughout Judah’s territory models the church’s dispersal through the Roman Empire (Acts 8:1-4: the persecution scattering the Jerusalem church throughout Judea and Samaria — “those who were scattered went about preaching the word”). What appears to be forced dispersal becomes priestly dispersal: the scattered community carries its priestly function wherever it goes.
16: A type: Ain with its pasturelands, Juttah with its pasturelands, Beth-shemesh with its pasturelands — nine cities total from the two tribes of Judah and Simeon. Beth-shemesh (“house of the sun”) is where the ark was returned from Philistine captivity (1 Samuel 6:13-15) — a Levitical city in Judah that received the ark’s return. The city named for the sun hosts the return of YHWH’s presence-object: a type of Christ as the Sun of Righteousness (Malachi 4:2) whose priestly city is wherever his presence returns to his people.
17: A type: four cities from the tribe of Benjamin — Gibeon with its pasturelands, Geba with its pasturelands. Gibeon is the great high place where Solomon will offer a thousand burnt offerings, and where YHWH will appear to him in a dream and grant him wisdom (1 Kings 3:4-5). The Levitical city of Gibeon becomes the site of the definitive royal theophany that shapes Solomon’s reign. The priestly city mediates the divine encounter that orients the king — the type of the Holy Spirit’s anointing of Christ at his baptism (Matthew 3:16-17: the Spirit descended as the priestly anointing of the royal Son).
18: A type: Anathoth with its pasturelands and Almon with its pasturelands — four cities of Benjamin for the Aaronites. Anathoth is the priestly city from which Jeremiah came (Jeremiah 1:1: “the words of Jeremiah son of Hilkiah, one of the priests at Anathoth in Benjamin”). The Levitical priestly city of Anathoth produces the weeping prophet who becomes the most Christological figure in the OT: the suffering righteous one rejected by his own people (Jeremiah 11:19: “like a gentle lamb led to the slaughter”; cf. Isaiah 53:7). Christ comes from a Levitical-Aaronite city’s prophetic tradition through Jeremiah.
19: A type: all the cities of the Aaronites — thirteen cities in all with their pasturelands. The summary of the Aaronite priestly cities — thirteen in number, spanning Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin — captures the high-priestly provision across the southern kingdom’s territory. The number thirteen is one more than twelve — the extra city beyond the tribal count, as Christ is the high priest who stands beyond and above the Levitical order (Hebrews 7:24: “he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever”).
20: A type: the remaining Kohathite Levites received cities from the tribe of Ephraim. Ephraim is the tribe of Joshua himself — the military leader whose name is embedded in the priestly city-allotments. Joshua the military leader and Eleazar the high priest together distributed the inheritance (Joshua 14:1); now their own tribal territories host the Levitical cities. The military-priestly partnership throughout the book of Joshua foreshadows the single Person who fulfills both roles: Christ as both the captain who fights for his people (Hebrews 2:10: “pioneer of their salvation”) and the high priest who intercedes for them (Hebrews 7:25).
21: A type: Shechem with its pasturelands in the hill country of Ephraim — a city of refuge — and Gezer with its pasturelands. Shechem is the covenant city where Abraham first built an altar (Genesis 12:6-7), where Jacob purchased land (Genesis 33:18-20), where the bones of Joseph will be buried (Joshua 24:32), and where the Ebal covenant ceremony was held (Joshua 8:30-35). The great covenant-gathering city is a city of refuge — the place of covenant renewal is also the place of asylum. Jesus’s encounter with the Samaritan woman at Jacob’s well in Sychar/Shechem (John 4:5-42) is the NT covenant-renewal at the type’s location: the true city of refuge meeting the woman fleeing her own failures.
22: A type: Kibzaim with its pasturelands and Beth-horon with its pasturelands — two more cities for the Kohathites in Ephraim. Beth-horon (upper and lower) is the strategic pass where Joshua’s famous long day occurred (Joshua 10:10-14: YHWH threw down great hailstones on the Amorites) — the pass where the most explicit divine intervention in the conquest happened. The Levitical city at Beth-horon is the priestly presence at the site of the most dramatic divine warrior-act: YHWH’s throwing of hailstones is the cosmic-warrior act that mirrors the final divine warrior-act of Revelation 16:21 (“great hailstones, each weighing about a hundred pounds, fell from heaven”).
23: A type: four cities from the tribe of Dan — Eltekeh and Gibbethon with their pasturelands. Dan’s territory is the coastal lowland near Philistia — the most contested boundary of the conquest. The Levitical presence in Dan’s territory was strategic: the tribe nearest the Philistines needed the priestly cities as covenantal anchors. Dan will eventually migrate north (Judg 18) and set up an idolatrous shrine — the failure of the Danite covenant identity. The Levitical cities in Dan represent the divine provision of priestly presence that was offered but ultimately not maintained, a type of the grace that can be squandered through covenant unfaithfulness.
24: A type: Aijalon with its pasturelands and Gath-rimmon with its pasturelands — additional Danite-Kohathite cities. Aijalon is the valley of the long day (Joshua 10:12: “moon, stand still over the Valley of Aijalon”) — the site of the cosmic stoppage where YHWH fought for Israel. The Levitical city in the valley of the long day connects priestly ministry with the most spectacular demonstration of YHWH’s cosmic sovereignty over creation in service of his covenant people. Second Peter Joshua 3:8 (“with the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day”) is the NT meditation on the same divine sovereignty over time that the long day at Aijalon embodied.
25: A type: two cities from the half-tribe of Manasseh — Taanach and Gath-rimmon. Taanach is the site of Deborah’s battle against Sisera (Judges 5:19: “the kings came and fought; at Taanach, by the waters of Megiddo, they got no spoils of silver”) — the Levitical city in the Jezreel Valley becomes the setting for Israel’s future deliverances through the judges. The priestly cities scattered through Israel function as covenant anchors that make possible the periodic redemptions of the judges period: the Levitical presence in Taanach represents the sustained covenant witness that grounds each subsequent act of divine deliverance.
26: A type: ten cities in all with their pasturelands for the rest of the Kohathite clans. The ten non-Aaronite Kohathite cities (Joshua 21:20-26) span four tribes — Ephraim, Dan, half-Manasseh — positioning the bearers of the ark and tabernacle furnishings throughout the central region of the land. The Kohathites at the center of the Levitical structure hold the most sacred objects of divine presence: the ark (type of Christ as the mercy seat, Romans 3:25: “God put forward as a propitiation by his blood”) and the table of showbread (type of Christ as the bread of life, John 6:35). Their ten cities are the priestly presence sustaining the land’s covenant life.
27: A type: the Gershonites received two cities — Golan in Bashan (a city of refuge) and Be-eshterah from the half-tribe of Manasseh. Golan in Bashan is the northernmost city of refuge — the asylum point at the far end of the land, in the territory of the defeated giant-king Og. The city of refuge at the edge of the conquered enemy’s domain is the type of Christ’s reach: the furthest, most seemingly unreachable place still has a city of asylum for those who flee to him. Hebrews 7:25: “he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him.”
28: A type: from the tribe of Issachar — Kishion with its pasturelands and Daberath with its pasturelands. Daberath is the town at the foot of Mount Tabor, where Deborah and Barak assembled before the battle against Sisera (Judges 4:6, 14). Mount Tabor is the probable location of the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-8; Mark 9:2-8; Luke 9:28-36 — the “high mountain apart”). The Levitical town at the foot of the mountain of divine glory connects the priestly-city tradition directly to the site of Christ’s eschatological self-revelation in the Synoptics.
29: A type: Jarmuth with its pasturelands and En-gannim with its pasturelands from Issachar. En-gannim (“spring of the gardens”) may be the same Jenin area that appears in the NT in the vicinity of the ten lepers’ healing (Luke 17:11-19: “on the way to Jerusalem he was passing along between Samaria and Galilee,” where one leper returned to give thanks). The Levitical spring-town in Issachar connects to the region of Jesus’s healing ministry, and the grateful Samaritan leper who returned — the foreigner who gave thanks — is the type of the Caleb-pattern: the outsider who comes back in faith and receives blessing.
30: A type: from the tribe of Asher — Mishal with its pasturelands and Abdon with its pasturelands. Asher’s territory stretches along the Phoenician coast — the northern boundary of the land with access to Tyre and Sidon. The Levitical cities in Asher place priestly presence at the interface with the great Phoenician city-states, the most culturally sophisticated neighbors of Israel. Jesus’s journey to the region of Tyre and Sidon (Mark 7:24-31) extends the covenant blessing beyond these Asherite boundary cities to the Gentile woman who worships him — the priestly-city border is expanded by Christ to encompass those beyond Israel’s geographical limits.
31: A type: Helkath with its pasturelands and Rehob with its pasturelands from Asher — four cities. Rehob is the northern boundary point of the Promised Land (Numbers 13:21: the spies went “from the wilderness of Zin to Rehob”) — the far northern limit reached in the first reconnaissance. The Levitical city at the land’s northern boundary marker is the type of the church’s presence at the frontier: Acts 1:8’s “ends of the earth” is the NT expansion of the frontier-city principle — the priestly community is placed even at the furthest boundary of the known world.
32: A type: from the tribe of Naphtali — Kedesh in Galilee (a city of refuge) with its pasturelands, Hammoth-dor with its pasturelands, Kartan with its pasturelands — three cities. Kedesh in Galilee is the city of refuge in the far north — the northernmost asylum city in Israel. Matthew 4:15-16 (quoting Isaiah 9:1-2): “the land of Zebulun and the land of Naphtali... Galilee of the Gentiles — the people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light.” The northernmost city of refuge (Kedesh-naphtali) is in the territory where Jesus brought the light of the gospel — the asylum-city of Joshua’s Galilee becomes the territory of Christ’s liberating ministry.
33: A type: all the cities of the Gershonites — thirteen cities with their pasturelands. The Gershonite total — thirteen cities, the same number as the Aaronites — ensures that the fabric-carriers of the tabernacle have the same scope of priestly presence as the high-priestly clan. In the body of Christ, every member’s function is necessary: “the body does not consist of one member but of many” (1 Corinthians 12:14). The Gershonite fabric-carriers have the same city-count as the Aaronite altar-ministers — different functions, equal provision, equal dispersal through the land.
34: A type: to the rest of the Levites — the Merarites — from the tribe of Zebulun: Jokneam with its pasturelands and Kartah with its pasturelands. Jokneam is the city near Mount Carmel, the site of Elijah’s confrontation with the prophets of Baal (1 Kings 18:19-40). The Levitical city at Carmel’s base positions priestly presence at what will become the decisive site of Israel’s Baal-rejection. The Carmel theophany — fire from heaven consuming the sacrifice — is the type of Pentecost (Acts 2:3: “tongues of fire... distributed and resting on each one of them”), where the Spirit’s fire confirms the true sacrifice (Christ) over against every idol.
35: A type: Dimnah with its pasturelands and Nahalal with its pasturelands from Zebulun — four cities total. The Zebulunite Merarite cities in the lower Galilee region encompass the area of Jesus’s hometown district (Nazareth is in the hills of lower Galilee, in Zebulun’s territory — Matthew 2:23: “he shall be called a Nazarene”). The structural-element-bearers of the tabernacle have their cities in the tribe that hosts the Word made flesh — the framework-bearers of YHWH’s dwelling place are located in the tribal territory of Christ’s human home.
36: A type: from the tribe of Reuben — Bezer with its pasturelands and Jahzah with its pasturelands. Bezer is the city of refuge for the eastern Transjordanian territory — the Reubenite asylum city. The city of refuge in Reuben’s territory is in the vicinity of the battle at Jahzah where Sihon was defeated (Numbers 21:23; Deuteronomy 2:32) — the site of the anti-kingdom’s defeat hosts the city of refuge. Christ’s death at the site of the greatest spiritual conflict becomes the source of asylum: the cross, the site of ultimate conflict, is where the refuge-city is founded for all who flee to him.
37: A type: Kedemoth with its pasturelands and Mephaath with its pasturelands from Reuben — four cities. Kedemoth is the wilderness from which Moses sent messengers to Sihon offering peace (Deuteronomy 2:26). The Levitical city at the peace-offer site is the priestly counterpart to the military mission: the priests inhabit the place from which peace was offered. In Christ, the gospel is the offer of peace from the one place where peace is possible: “In Christ God was reconciling the world to himself... and entrusting to us the message of reconciliation” (2 Corinthians 5:19). The Merarite priests at Kedemoth are the type of the ministers of reconciliation.
38: A type: from the tribe of Gad — Ramoth in Gilead (a city of refuge) with its pasturelands and Mahanaim with its pasturelands. Ramoth in Gilead is one of the six cities of refuge and also the site of two major battles: (1) Ahab is mortally wounded at Ramoth-gilead (1 Kings 22:34-36); (2) Jehu is anointed king there by Elisha’s prophetic messenger (2 Kings 9:1-6). The city of refuge becomes the site of royal anointing and judgment. Christ as the anointed king (the Messiah, the Christ — the anointed one) is crowned through the judgment-event of the cross: anointed king by the very act that wounds him mortally (Philippians 2:8-9: “obedient to the point of death... therefore God has highly exalted him”).
39: A type: Heshbon with its pasturelands and Jazer with its pasturelands from Gad — four cities. Heshbon was Sihon’s capital — the anti-kingdom’s administrative center — now a Levitical city. The priests inhabit the former enemy capital, transforming the seat of resistance into a place of covenant service. Second Corinthians Joshua 10:4-5: “we destroy arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and take every thought captive to obey Christ” — the Levitical occupation of Heshbon is the type of Christ’s priestly presence transforming former strongholds of the enemy into centers of covenant worship.
40: A type: all the cities allotted to the Merarites — twelve cities in total. The Merarite twelve matches the twelve tribes — one city per tribe in the structural-bearing clan’s allotment, ensuring that the framework of YHWH’s dwelling is present throughout the entire covenant community. In the new covenant, the Holy Spirit indwells every believer (1 Corinthians 3:16: “you are God’s temple and God’s Spirit dwells in you”) — the Merarite framework-presence, distributed one city per tribe, is the type of the Spirit’s distributed presence in every member of the new covenant assembly.
41: A type: the total — forty-eight cities with their pasturelands for the Levites within the possession of the people of Israel. The number forty-eight is the complete Levitical city count: four Levitical clans (Aaronite Kohathites 13 + non-Aaronite Kohathites 10 + Gershonites 13 + Merarites 12 = 48). Forty-eight cities distributed through all twelve tribes — four per tribe — ensures no tribal territory is without a priestly city. The mathematical completeness of the Levitical city distribution (every tribe, four cities each) is the type of the Spirit’s comprehensive provision: no member of the body of Christ is without access to the priestly ministry of the word and intercession (Hebrews 4:16: “let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace”).
42: A type: each of these cities had its pasturelands surrounding it — so for all these cities. The migrash (pastureland) provision is the Levites’ equivalent of the tribal “fields and villages” given to Caleb: the priestly city includes not only the urban center but the surrounding agricultural land to sustain the priestly household. The complete provision — city and pastureland — is the type of Christ’s complete provision: not only access to his priestly presence (the city) but the daily sustenance to live in it (the pastureland). Matthew 6:31-33: “do not be anxious... your heavenly Father knows you need all these things. Seek first the kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you.”
43: A direct revelation: “Thus YHWH gave to Israel all the land that he swore to give to their fathers. And they took possession of it and settled there.” This verse is the first of the book’s two great summary-fulfillment statements (the second is v45). The land-gift is stated in the same terms as the patriarchal promise (Genesis 12:7; Joshua 13:15; Joshua 15:18-21; Joshua 17:8; Joshua 26:3; Joshua 28:13) — what was sworn is now given. Galatians 3:16-18: “the promises were made to Abraham and to his offspring... God gave it to Abraham by a promise” — the inheritance-by-promise is not the Sinaitic law but the covenant with Abraham, fulfilled in Christ who is the true offspring (Galatians 3:16: “not ‘and to offsprings,’ as referring to many, but as to one, ‘and to your offspring,’ who is Christ”). The land-fulfillment of Joshua 21:43 is the type; the inheritance-fulfillment in Christ is the antitype.
44: A direct revelation: “And YHWH gave them rest on every side just as he had sworn to their fathers. Not one of all their enemies withstood them, for YHWH gave all their enemies into their hands.” The rest-gift on every side is the fulfillment of the holy-war promise (Deuteronomy 3:20: “until YHWH gives rest to your brothers, as he has given rest to you”; Joshua 1:13, 15). Hebrews 4:9-10: “there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God’s rest has also rested from his works as God did from his.” The rest at Canaan’s conquest is a real but provisional fulfillment: the complete rest, from which no enemy can disturb, is the eschatological Sabbath-rest that Christ gives (Matthew 11:28-29: “come to me... and I will give you rest... you will find rest for your souls”).
